questions.prompt: A legacy OEM has 15 EV models, each at 30-80K units/year. They evaluate gigacasting for their underbody. Right call?

choices.text: Probably not at current volumes. Gigacasting breakeven volume is roughly 300K+ units/year per program. Die capex is sunk per design — at 30-80K, the capex doesn't amortize. Right call: stay with stamping for now; revisit if they consolidate to fewer, higher-volume platforms.

questions.explanation: Gigacasting is a high-fixed-cost, low-variable-cost play. It wins at scale; loses below scale. For a multi-model OEM with sub-100K volumes per program, stamping economics still win. Platform consolidation could change the math.

questions.prompt: What's a real downside of gigacasting beyond production economics?

choices.text: Design rigidity. Casting dies cost millions to tens of millions; changes require new dies. Mid-cycle refreshes are constrained. Country-specific variants are expensive. Stamping is much more forgiving for iteration. Plus: supply chain concentration (presses, alloys, dies from few suppliers) and repair-cost externality on owners.

questions.explanation: Gigacasting trades flexibility for cost. Once committed to a die, design changes are very expensive. Plus supply concentration and repair-cost externalities. The total cost picture isn't just production economics; it includes design agility and lifecycle costs.
